name: Irish Full Breakfast
steps: Fry the bacon and sausage together until browned.
Warm the diced tomatoes in the same pan.
Fry the eggs and serve everything together.
steps_detailed: Fry the bacon and sausage together in the butter over medium heat until well browned, 6-8 minutes.
Warm the diced tomatoes in the same pan for 2-3 minutes.
Fry the eggs in the rendered fat to your preferred doneness, seasoning with black pepper.
Serve everything together on one plate with toast - the full Irish breakfast, a weekend institution.

name: Irish Soda Bread
steps: Mix the flour, baking powder, and a pinch of salt.
Stir in the sour cream (thinned with the milk, as a buttermilk stand-in) until a soft dough forms.
Shape into a round, score a cross on top, and bake at 400F for 30-35 minutes until golden and hollow-sounding.
steps_detailed: Mix the flour, baking powder, and a good pinch of salt in a bowl.

Thin the sour cream with the milk to mimic buttermilk's tang and consistency, then stir into the dry ingredients along with the melted butter until a soft, slightly sticky dough forms - don't overwork it.
Shape into a round loaf on a floured surface and cut a deep cross into the top.
Bake at 400F (200C) for 32-38 minutes until deeply golden and it sounds hollow when tapped on the bottom.
Best served warm, sliced thick, with butter - a simple bread with no yeast, leavened entirely by the baking powder reacting with the acidic buttermilk.

name: Irish Porridge

ingredients.ingredient: Oats (Includes foods for USDA's Food Distribution Program)

steps: Simmer the oats in the milk (or water) for 5-7 minutes, stirring, until thick and creamy.
Drizzle with honey before serving.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Oats (Includes foods for USDA's Food Distribution Program)

steps_detailed: Combine the oats, milk, and cinnamon in a saucepan.
Simmer over medium-low heat, stirring often, 6-8 minutes until thick and creamy.
Drizzle with honey and serve hot - a simple, warming Irish breakfast staple, traditionally made with just oats, water, and salt.

name: Irish Stew

ingredients.note: or lamb stew meat if available

steps: Brown the lamb with the onion.
Add the potato, carrot, and enough water to cover, and simmer, covered, 1-1.25 hours until everything is tender.
Season and serve hot.

ingredients_full.note: or lamb stew meat if available

steps_detailed: Brown the lamb with the onion over medium-high heat, 6-8 minutes.
Add the potato, carrot, bay leaf, oregano, and beef broth, adding water if needed to just cover.
Cover and simmer over low heat 1-1.5 hours until the lamb is completely tender and the potato has partially broken down, thickening the stew naturally.
Remove the bay leaf and serve hot with soda bread - Ireland's most iconic dish, traditionally a simple, humble farmhouse meal.

name: Irish Colcannon
steps: Mash the boiled potato with the butter and milk until smooth.
Fold in the cooked cabbage.
Serve hot with an extra pat of butter.
steps_detailed: Saute the onion in a little of the butter until soft, then cook the cabbage with it until tender.
Mash the boiled potato with the remaining butter and milk until smooth and creamy.
Fold in the cabbage and onion, and season with black pepper.
Serve hot, traditionally with a well in the center filled with melted butter - a beloved Irish comfort food, especially popular at Halloween.

name: Irish Boxty

ingredients.note: half boiled and mashed, half raw and grated

steps: Mix the mashed and grated potato with the flour and a pinch of salt into a thick batter.
Fry spoonfuls flattened into pancakes in the butter, 3-4 minutes per side, until golden.
Serve warm.

ingredients_full.note: half boiled and mashed, half raw and grated

steps_detailed: Squeeze excess liquid from the grated raw potato.
Mix the mashed and grated potato with the flour, baking powder, and milk into a thick, cohesive batter.
Heat the butter in a skillet over medium heat and fry spoonfuls of the batter, flattened into pancakes, 3-4 minutes per side until golden brown.
Serve warm, traditionally with butter or alongside a full breakfast - 'boxty on the griddle, boxty in the pan' is a well-known Irish rhyme about this potato pancake.

name: Irish Dublin Coddle

ingredients.note: 1.5 servings, sliced, as a sausage stand-in

steps: Layer the sausage, bacon, potato, and onion in a pot with enough water to just cover.
Simmer, covered, 45-50 minutes until everything is tender.
Serve hot with bread.

ingredients_full.note: 2 servings, sliced, as a sausage stand-in

steps_detailed: Layer the sliced sausage, chopped bacon, potato, and onion in a pot, seasoning with black pepper and oregano between layers.
Add water just to cover the layers.
Cover and simmer over low heat 50-60 minutes until the potatoes are very tender and the broth has taken on flavor from the meats.
Serve hot in bowls with crusty bread - a humble Dublin classic, traditionally made from whatever was on hand.

name: Irish Shepherd's Pie
steps: Brown the lamb with the carrot.
Stir in the peas and spread the mixture in a small baking dish.
Top with the mashed potato and bake at 400F for 20-25 minutes until golden on top.
steps_detailed: Brown the lamb with the onion and carrot over medium-high heat, 8-10 minutes.
Add the beef broth and oregano, and simmer 5-6 minutes until slightly thickened, then stir in the peas.
Spread the lamb mixture evenly in a small buttered baking dish.
Mash the boiled potato with the butter until smooth, then spread evenly over the lamb layer, running a fork over the top for texture.
Bake at 400F (200C) for 22-28 minutes until the top is golden and the filling is bubbling at the edges - traditionally made with lamb, distinguishing it from cottage pie's beef.

name: Irish Bacon and Cabbage

ingredients.note: 1.5 servings, or bacon joint if available

steps: Simmer the bacon in water 20-25 minutes.
Add the potato and cabbage, and simmer 15-18 more minutes until tender.
Slice the bacon and serve with the vegetables.

ingredients_full.note: 2 servings, or bacon joint if available

steps_detailed: Simmer the bacon in water to cover for 20-25 minutes.
Add the potato and carrot, and simmer 15 minutes.
Add the cabbage and continue simmering 10-12 minutes until all the vegetables are tender.
Slice the bacon and serve with the vegetables and a little of the cooking broth, seasoned with black pepper - a simple, classic Irish Sunday dinner.

name: Irish Beef and Guinness Stew

ingredients.ingredient: Beef, chuck, short ribs, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 0" fat, all grades, raw

steps: Brown the beef with the onion.
Add the broth (a splash of dark beer adds the traditional depth if you have it) and carrot, and simmer, covered, 1.5-2 hours until tender.
Serve with mashed potato.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Beef, chuck, short ribs, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 0" fat, all grades, raw

steps_detailed: Brown the beef well in batches, then set aside.
Saute the onion and garlic in the same pot for 5-6 minutes until softened, then stir in the tomato paste.
Return the beef, add the beef broth, carrot, and bay leaf (a cup of stout beer traditionally goes in here too, for the dish's name and characteristic depth).
Cover and simmer over low heat 1.5-2 hours until the beef is completely tender and the sauce has thickened.
Remove the bay leaf and serve with mashed potato - a hearty pub classic found across Ireland.

name: Irish Corned Beef and Cabbage

ingredients.ingredient: Beef, chuck, short ribs, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 0" fat, all grades, raw
ingredients.note: well-salted, as a corned beef stand-in

steps: Season the beef generously with extra salt and simmer in water 1.5-2 hours until very tender.
Add the potato and carrot, and simmer 15 minutes, then add the cabbage and simmer 10-12 more minutes.
Slice the beef and serve with the vegetables.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Beef, chuck, short ribs, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 0" fat, all grades, raw
ingredients_full.note: well-salted, as a corned beef stand-in

steps_detailed: Season the beef generously with extra salt (mimicking the brine of true corned beef) and black pepper, and let sit 15 minutes.
Simmer the beef with the onion and bay leaf in water to cover for 1.5-2.5 hours until completely fork-tender.
Add the potato and carrot, and simmer 15 minutes.
Add the cabbage wedges and continue simmering 10-15 minutes until all the vegetables are tender.
Remove the bay leaf, slice the beef against the grain, and serve with the vegetables - beloved in Irish-American tradition especially, and closely associated with St. Patrick's Day.

name: Irish Cheese and Brown Bread
steps: Mix the flour, baking powder, sour cream, and a splash of water into a soft dough, and bake into a small loaf at 400F for 25 minutes.
Slice and top with the cheese.
steps_detailed: Mix the flour, baking powder, sour cream, and milk into a soft dough, then shape into a small loaf.
Bake at 400F (200C) for 25-30 minutes until golden and cooked through.
Let cool slightly, slice, butter, and top with the cheese - a simple, satisfying Irish snack.

name: Irish Sausage Rolls
steps: Make a simple pastry from the flour, butter, and cold water.
Wrap the pastry around the sausage and bake at 375F for 25-30 minutes until golden.
Slice and serve warm.
steps_detailed: Make a simple pastry from the flour, butter, a pinch of salt, and cold water, rolling into a rectangle.
Lay the chopped sausage along the center and wrap the pastry around it, sealing the seam.
Brush with the egg wash and bake at 375F (190C) for 25-32 minutes until the pastry is golden and flaky.
Slice into pieces and serve warm - a beloved party and pub snack across Ireland.

name: Irish Potato Farls
steps: Mix the mashed potato with the flour and a pinch of salt into a firm dough.
Roll out and cut into triangles (farls).
Fry in the butter, 3-4 minutes per side, until golden.
steps_detailed: Mix the mashed potato with the flour, black pepper, and a good pinch of salt into a firm, workable dough.
Roll out to about 1/2 inch thick and cut into a circle, then cut the circle into four triangular farls.
Heat the butter in a skillet over medium heat and fry the farls 3-4 minutes per side until golden and slightly crisp.
Serve warm, traditionally as part of a full Irish breakfast.

name: Irish Champ

ingredients.note: finely chopped, as a scallion stand-in

steps: Warm the milk with the chopped onion (traditionally scallions/spring onions) over low heat for 3-4 minutes until fragrant and just simmering.
Mash the boiled potato with half the butter until smooth.
Stir in the warm milk and onion mixture, and season with black pepper.
Serve hot with a well in the center filled with the remaining melted butter - simple, comforting, and deeply traditional.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Onions, spring or scallions (includes tops and bulb), raw
ingredients_full.note: finely chopped, as a scallion stand-in

steps_detailed: Warm the milk with the chopped onion (traditionally scallions/spring onions) over low heat for 3-4 minutes until fragrant and just simmering.
Mash the boiled potato with half the butter until smooth.
Stir in the warm milk and onion mixture, and season with black pepper.
Serve hot with a well in the center filled with the remaining melted butter - simple, comforting, and deeply traditional.
Real scallions are used here now, giving the sharper, more specific flavor the dish actually calls for rather than yellow onion's milder substitute.

name: Irish Apple Cake

ingredients.ingredient: Apples, raw, gala, with skin (Includes foods for USDA's Food Distribution Program)

steps: Cream the butter and sugar, then work in the flour to form a soft batter.
Fold in the diced apple.
Bake at 350F for 30-35 minutes until golden and a toothpick comes out clean.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Apples, raw, gala, with skin (Includes foods for USDA's Food Distribution Program)

steps_detailed: Cream the butter and sugar until light and fluffy, then beat in the egg.
Sift together the flour, baking powder, and cinnamon, and fold into the batter until just combined.
Fold in the diced apple.
Pour into a small buttered baking dish and bake at 350F (175C) for 32-40 minutes until golden and a toothpick comes out clean.
Serve warm, traditionally with custard or cream - a simple, homey Irish classic.

name: Irish Barmbrack

ingredients.ingredient: Raisins, dark, seedless (Includes foods for USDA's Food Distribution Program)

steps: Soak the raisins in warm tea for at least 30 minutes, then drain, reserving the tea.
Mix the flour, sugar, and baking powder with the soaked raisins and enough of the reserved tea to form a thick batter.
Bake at 325F for 40-45 minutes until a toothpick comes out clean.

ingredients_full.ingredient: Raisins, dark, seedless (Includes foods for USDA's Food Distribution Program)

steps_detailed: Soak the raisins in warm strong tea for at least 1 hour, or overnight, then drain well, reserving a little of the tea.
Beat the egg with the brown sugar, then stir in the flour, baking powder, and cinnamon along with the soaked raisins and a splash of the reserved tea to form a thick, spoonable batter.
Pour into a small buttered loaf pan.
Bake at 325F (165C) for 45-55 minutes until a toothpick comes out clean.
Traditionally baked for Halloween with small charms hidden inside to predict the future - a beloved Irish tea bread.

name: Irish Bread and Butter Pudding

ingredients.ingredient: 365 (Whole Foods) Graham Crackers Flavored With Honey, Honey

steps: Butter the crackers and arrange in a small baking dish.
Whisk the egg, milk, and sugar together and pour over.
Bake at 350F for 30-35 minutes until set and golden on top.

ingredients_full.ingredient: 365 (Whole Foods) Graham Crackers Flavored With Honey, Honey
Raisins, dark, seedless (Includes foods for USDA's Food Distribution Program)

steps_detailed: Butter the crackers (standing in for slightly stale bread) and layer them in a small baking dish, scattering the raisins between layers.
Whisk the eggs, milk, sugar, and cinnamon together until smooth, and pour evenly over the bread, pressing gently so it soaks in.
Let sit 10 minutes so the custard absorbs before baking.
Bake at 350F (175C) for 32-40 minutes until set and golden on top.
Serve warm - simple, humble, and a great way to use up leftover bread, much loved across Ireland and Britain alike.

name: Irish Whiskey Cream Sauce
steps: Whisk the sour cream, sugar, and milk together until smooth.
Warm gently and serve over cake or fruit.
steps_detailed: Whisk the sour cream, brown sugar, milk, and vanilla together until smooth.
Warm gently in a small saucepan over low heat, stirring, until slightly thickened, about 3-4 minutes - don't let it boil.
Serve warm over apple cake, bread pudding, or fresh fruit, echoing the flavor of a whiskey cream liqueur without the alcohol.

name: Irish Mustard Sauce
steps: Whisk the mustard, honey, and sour cream together until smooth.
Serve alongside ham, sausages, or boiled bacon.
steps_detailed: Whisk the mustard, honey, sour cream, and black pepper together until smooth and well combined.
Chill briefly before serving alongside ham, sausages, or boiled bacon and cabbage.
